hoses: Hydrant supply hose from hydrant to Engine 181 intake
Ladder supply line A: 200 ft of 2.5-inch hose from Engine 181 discharge to ladder truck intake
Ladder supply line B: 200 ft of 2.5-inch hose from Engine 181 discharge to ladder truck intake
nozzles: Aerial ladder pipe master stream, 750 GPM @ 80 PSI
appliances: Ladder pipe nozzle at the tip of the aerial ladder
sceneElements.elevation: Ladder pipe nozzle is 60 ft above Engine 181, requiring 30 PSI elevation pressure

problems.title: Total Ladder Pipe Flow
problems.question: Engine 181 is supplying a ladder pipe flowing 750 GPM. What is the total flow for the ladder pipe operation?
formulaBreakdown: The ladder pipe operation is flowing 750 GPM.
problems.instructorExplanation: The total flow is the amount being delivered through the aerial ladder pipe. In this operation, the ladder pipe flow is 750 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: Do not divide the ladder pipe flow unless the question asks for flow per supply line.

problems.title: Flow Per Ladder Supply Line
problems.question: Engine 181 supplies the ladder truck with two equal supply lines. The ladder pipe is flowing 750 GPM total. How much flow is carried by each supply line?
formulaBreakdown: Flow per line = total ladder pipe flow / number of supply lines.
problems.instructorExplanation: The two ladder supply lines split the total flow evenly. Each line carries 375 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: Do not calculate friction loss using 750 GPM in each line. Each supply line carries half of the total ladder pipe flow.

problems.title: Ladder Supply Line Friction Loss
problems.question: Each ladder supply line is 200 ft of 2.5-inch hose flowing 375 GPM. What is the friction loss in each supply line?
formulaBreakdown: FL = C x (GPM / 100)^2 x hose length in hundreds.
problems.instructorExplanation: Each supply line carries 375 GPM. At 375 GPM through 200 ft of 2.5-inch hose, friction loss rounds to 55 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add the friction loss from both equal supply lines together. Calculate one supply path because the lines are parallel.

problems.title: Ladder Pipe Elevation Pressure
problems.question: The ladder pipe nozzle is 60 ft above Engine 181. What elevation pressure must be added?
formulaBreakdown: Elevation pressure = elevation height x 0.5 PSI per foot.
problems.instructorExplanation: The ladder pipe is above Engine 181, so elevation pressure must be added. A 60 ft elevation gain requires 30 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not subtract elevation pressure for an elevated ladder pipe. Pressure must be added for uphill elevation.

problems.title: Ladder Pipe Pump Pressure
problems.question: Engine 181 supplies the ladder truck with two 200 ft 2.5-inch supply lines. The ladder pipe flows 750 GPM total, each supply line carries 375 GPM, friction loss is 55 PSI, nozzle pressure is 80 PSI, elevation is 30 PSI, and ladder waterway appliance loss is 25 PSI. What pump discharge pressure should Engine 181 set?
formulaBreakdown: PP = FL + NP + elevation + appliance loss.
Friction loss per supply line = 55 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: For a ladder pipe supplied through two equal lines, calculate one supply path. Add supply line friction loss, ladder pipe nozzle pressure, elevation pressure, and ladder waterway appliance loss. Engine 181 should pump 190 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add the friction loss of both blue supply lines together. Parallel supply lines split the flow, so one supply path governs the pump pressure.

problems.title: Ladder Truck Supply Hose Count
problems.question: How many supply hoses from Engine 181 feed the ladder truck?
formulaBreakdown: The scene shows two supply hoses from Engine 181 to the ladder truck.
problems.instructorExplanation: The hydrant supplies Engine 181, and Engine 181 supplies the ladder truck with two separate supply hoses.
problems.explainMistake: Do not count the yellow hydrant supply hose as a ladder truck supply hose. The ladder truck is fed by the two blue hoses from Engine 181.
